saida_bruta:
As the boys climbed the hill, she watched them pause and whisper together. “You ask her, Niel.”

“You should, George. She visits your house all the time, and she hardly knows me well enough to speak.”

When they stopped before the three steps leading to the front porch, Mrs. Forrester appeared in the doorway and smiled graciously, holding one of the pink roses.

“Good morning, boys. Going on a picnic?”

George Adams came forward and gravely removed his large straw hat. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we please fish and wade in the marsh and eat our lunch in the grove?”

“Of course. It is a beautiful day. How long has school been closed? Don’t you miss it? I’m certain Niel does. Judge Pommeroy says he is very diligent.”

The boys laughed, and Niel seemed uncomfortable.

“Off you go, and make sure you don’t leave the pasture gate open. Mr. Forrester dislikes having the cattle wander onto his blue grass.”

The boys walked silently around the house toward the gate into the grove, then dashed shouting down the grassy hillsides beneath the tall trees. Mrs. Forrester watched from the kitchen window until they vanished beyond the crest of the hill. Then she spoke to her Bohemian cook.

“Mary, while you are baking this morning, make a pan of cookies for those boys. I shall bring them down when they are eating lunch.”

saida_bruta:
As the boys came up the hill, she saw them stop and confer in low voices. “You ask her, Niel.”

“You ought to, George. You go to her house all the time, and she scarcely knows me well enough to talk.”

When they halted at the three steps leading to the front porch, Mrs. Forrester came into the doorway and smiled kindly, carrying one of the pink roses.

“Good morning, boys. Are you going on a picnic?”

George Adams stepped forward and solemnly took off his broad straw hat. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish and wade in the marsh and have our lunch in the grove?”

“Certainly. It is a lovely day. How long has school been out? Don’t you miss it? I am sure Niel does. Judge Pommeroy says he is very industrious.”

The boys laughed, and Niel looked embarrassed.

“Run along, and be careful not to leave the pasture gate open. Mr. Forrester does not like the cattle straying into his blue grass.”

The boys went quietly around the house toward the gate opening into the grove, then ran shouting down the green slopes beneath the tall trees. Mrs. Forrester watched them from the kitchen window until they disappeared over the hill’s crest. Then she addressed her Bohemian cook.

“Mary, while you are baking this morning, make some cookies for those boys. I will carry them down when they are having lunch.”

“You ought to, George. You go to her house all the time, and she barely knows me well enough to talk with.”

When they reached the three steps before the porch, Mrs. Forrester came into the doorway, smiling kindly with a pink rose in her hand.

“Good morning, boys. Are you going on a picnic?”

George Adams stepped forward and solemnly lifted off his broad straw hat. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. Could we fish and wade in the marsh, and have our lunch in the grove?”

“Certainly. It is such a lovely day. How long have you been out of school? Don’t you miss it? I am sure Niel does. Judge Pommeroy tells me he is a very hardworking boy.”

The boys began to laugh, and Niel reddened awkwardly.

“Now be off with you, and be careful not to leave the pasture gate standing open. Mr. Forrester does not like the cattle straying into his bluegrass.”

The boys went quietly around the house toward the gate leading into the grove, then dashed away, shouting down the green slopes under the tall trees. Mrs. Forrester watched them from the kitchen window until they disappeared over the hill’s crest. Then she addressed her Bohemian cook.

When they reached the three steps before the porch, Mrs. Forrester stood in the doorway, smiling kindly, with a pink rose in her hand.

“Good morning, boys. Are you going out for a picnic?”

George Adams stepped ahead and solemnly took off his broad straw hat. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish and wade in the marsh, and have our lunch in the grove?”

“Certainly. It is a lovely day. How long have you been out of school? Don’t you miss it? I am sure Niel does. Judge Pommeroy tells me he is a very hardworking boy.”

The boys burst into laughter, and Niel reddened awkwardly.

“Now be off, and remember not to leave the pasture gate open. Mr. Forrester does not like the cattle getting into his bluegrass.”

The boys slipped quietly around the house toward the grove gate, then dashed away, shouting down the green slope under the high trees. Mrs. Forrester watched from the kitchen window until they disappeared over the hilltop. Then she addressed her Bohemian cook.

“Mary, while you are baking this morning, prepare some cookies for those boys. I will take them down when they have lunch.”

As the boys climbed the hill, she saw them stop and whisper together. “You ask her, Niel.”

“You ask, George. You are always at her house, and she scarcely knows me well enough to speak to me.”

When they came to the porch steps, Mrs. Forrester was standing in the doorway, smiling, with a pink rose in her hand.

“Good morning, boys. Are you planning a picnic?”

George Adams moved forward and gravely removed his wide straw hat. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish and wade in the marsh, and eat our lunch in the grove?”

“Of course. It is such a beautiful day. How long has school been over? Do you not miss it? I am certain Niel does. Judge Pommeroy says he is a very diligent boy.”

The boys broke into laughter, and Niel flushed with embarrassment.

“Go along, then, and do not forget to leave the pasture gate shut. Mr. Forrester does not want the cattle wandering into his bluegrass.”

The boys went around the house toward the grove gate, then dashed off, yelling as they hurried down the green slope beneath the tall trees. Mrs. Forrester watched from the kitchen window until they vanished over the hill. Then she spoke to her Bohemian cook.

“Mary, while you are baking this morning, make some cookies for those boys. I will carry them down when they stop for lunch.”
